title: Theory of Magnetism: Domains, Materials, and Flux
narrationScript: Why does a steel shaft grab magnetic particles while an aluminum housing ignores them completely? The answer lives at the atomic scale, and it decides which parts you can test and which you cannot. By the end of this lesson you will know what a magnetic domain is, which three families of materials exist, what flux density means when you read it on a meter, and the simple law of attraction and repulsion that makes particles pile up on a crack. Start inside the atom. Every spinning electron behaves like a tiny magnet. In most materials those tiny magnets point every which way and cancel out. But in iron, nickel, and cobalt, something special happens. Neighboring atoms lock their magnetism together over small microscopic regions called domains. Each domain is a tiny volume where huge numbers of atoms all point the same direction, so every domain is a little magnet that is fully magnetized all the time. Then why is not every piece of steel a magnet? Because the domains themselves point in random directions, and across the whole part they cancel each other out. Here is the key idea. An unmagnetized part is not a part with no magnetism inside. It is a part whose internal magnetism is scrambled. Now apply a magnetizing force, say from a coil or a current shot. The domains respond. Domains that happen to align with the applied field grow at the expense of their neighbors, and as the force rises, whole domains rotate into line. The part develops a net field of its own, with a north pole and a south pole. Push hard enough and essentially every domain is aligned. That condition is called saturation, and beyond it more magnetizing force buys you almost nothing. Now take the force away. Some domains relax back to random, but many stay lined up, and the part keeps a residual field. Steel remembers. That memory is the basis of the residual inspection method, and it is the reason demagnetization exists. We will trace the full loop of that behavior in the next lesson. Next, materials. We sort everything into three families by how it responds to a field. First, ferromagnetic materials. Iron, nickel, cobalt, and most of their alloys, including the carbon and low alloy steels you will test every week. These have domains and are strongly attracted to a magnet. Their permeability, meaning the ease with which they accept flux, can be hundreds or even thousands of times that of air. These are the only materials magnetic particle testing can examine. Second, paramagnetic materials, like aluminum, titanium, and the three hundred series austenitic stainless steels. They are attracted so weakly you need laboratory equipment to notice, with permeability barely above one. Third, diamagnetic materials, like copper, bismuth, and gold. They are actually repelled by a field, just as feebly. For our purposes, paramagnetic and diamagnetic mean the same thing. No magnetic particle testing. Choose penetrant or eddy current instead. One trap worth calling out. Stainless steel is not one material. The four hundred series martensitic grades are ferromagnetic and testable, while a three oh four austenitic grade is not. Check the grade, not the word stainless. Temperature matters too. Heat iron past its Curie point, about seven hundred seventy degrees Celsius, and thermal agitation destroys the domain alignment. The steel becomes paramagnetic until it cools back down. Now put numbers on the field. We picture flux as lines, and flux density is how many of those lines pass through a unit of area. Its symbol is B, measured in gauss or tesla, and ten thousand gauss equals one tesla. For scale, the earth's field is about half a gauss. The magnetizing force we apply gets the symbol H, measured in oersteds. Divide flux density by magnetizing force, B over H, and you get permeability. Same applied force, wildly different flux. That ratio is why steel works and aluminum never will. Finally, the law of magnetism. Like poles repel, unlike poles attract. Every magnet has both a north and a south pole. Cut a bar magnet in half and you do not get a separate north and a separate south. You get two complete smaller magnets. Poles always come in pairs. And the force between poles is strongest up close, falling away fast with distance. Now connect that to the job. A crack in a magnetized part creates a tiny north and south pole pair at its edges. Those poles attract ferromagnetic particles exactly the way a magnet grabs iron filings, and because magnetic force fades quickly with distance, the particles are held tightly right at the crack and only loosely everywhere else. That is why a good indication forms sharp and crisp directly over the flaw instead of smearing across the part.
imagePrompt: Macro photograph of fine iron filings standing in curved arcs on a polished steel bar between the poles of a heavy industrial magnet on a laboratory bench, with untouched aluminum and copper coupons lying beside it.

sections.heading: Magnetism Starts Inside the Atom
sections.body:
Every spinning electron behaves like a microscopic magnet. In most materials these atomic magnets point in random directions and cancel one another, so the bulk material shows no magnetism at all.

In a few materials — **iron, nickel, and cobalt** — adjacent atoms lock their magnetic moments together across small regions called **domains**. A domain is a microscopic volume in which enormous numbers of atoms all point the same way, so every domain is a tiny, fully magnetized permanent magnet. Domains exist whether or not the part has ever seen a magnetizing current; what changes is their *arrangement*.

This is the foundation fact of the whole method: a so-called unmagnetized steel part is not empty of magnetism — it is full of magnetism that is **scrambled**. Each domain points its own way, the fields cancel across the part, and the net external field is zero. Magnetizing a part does not create magnetism from nothing; it organizes magnetism that was already there.

sections.heading: Domain Theory: From Scrambled to Aligned
sections.body:
Apply a magnetizing force — from a coil, a yoke, or a current shot — and the domains respond in two ways:

- Domains already aligned with the applied field **grow** at the expense of less favorably oriented neighbors.
- As the force increases, whole domains **rotate** to line up with the field.

The part now produces a net external field with a north and a south pole. Keep raising the magnetizing force and eventually essentially all domains are aligned: that is **saturation**. Beyond saturation, more magnetizing force produces almost no additional flux in the part — a fact with practical consequences, because over-driving a part past saturation just forces flux out of the surface and creates false, fuzzy particle backgrounds.

Remove the force and the domains do **not** all relax. Some snap back to random orientations, but many stay aligned, leaving a **residual field**. Steel remembers. That memory makes the residual inspection method possible on high-retentivity alloys, and it is the entire reason demagnetization is part of your job. The next lesson traces this behavior quantitatively as the hysteresis loop.

sections.caption: Domains in an unmagnetized part point randomly and cancel; an applied magnetizing force aligns them, and the part develops poles of its own.

sections.heading: Three Families of Materials
sections.body:
Every material falls into one of three classes by its response to a magnetic field:

- **Ferromagnetic** — iron, nickel, cobalt, and most of their alloys, including carbon and low-alloy steels. They have domains, are strongly attracted to a magnet, and accept flux with **permeability** hundreds to thousands of times that of air. *These are the only materials MT can examine.*
- **Paramagnetic** — aluminum, titanium, and austenitic (300-series) stainless steels. Attracted so weakly it takes a laboratory to measure; relative permeability barely above 1. No domains, no MT.
- **Diamagnetic** — copper, bismuth, gold. Feebly *repelled* by a field; relative permeability slightly below 1. No MT.

Two field-proven cautions. First, **"stainless" is not a material class**: martensitic 400-series grades are ferromagnetic and testable, while austenitic 304/316 is not — check the grade, not the word. Second, ferromagnetism is temperature-limited: above the **Curie point** (about 770 °C / 1,418 °F for iron) thermal agitation destroys domain alignment and the steel behaves paramagnetically until it cools.

sections.caption: The three magnetic material classes and what they mean for the MT technician.
rows: Iron, nickel, cobalt, carbon & low-alloy steels, 400-series stainless
Yes — the only class MT can test
No — use PT, ET, or another method

sections.heading: Flux Density B and Magnetizing Force H
sections.body:
To work with fields quantitatively you need two quantities and the ratio between them.

**Flux density, B**, is the concentration of flux — how many flux lines pass through a unit of area. It is measured in **gauss** or **tesla**, and **10,000 gauss = 1 tesla**. For scale: the earth's field is about 0.5 gauss, and a Hall-effect meter held against a properly magnetized part during an MT shot commonly reads a tangential field on the order of 30–60 gauss (typical procedure values — your written procedure governs).

**Magnetizing force, H**, is the field you *apply* — the effort, set by your amperage and geometry. It is measured in **oersteds** (or amperes per meter in SI).

The ratio between result and effort is **permeability**: µ = B / H. A material that develops 12,000 gauss under 60 oersteds has a permeability of 200; air under the same force develops 60 gauss — permeability 1. Same effort, two hundred times the flux. Permeability is why flux stays in steel, why MT works on steel, and why no amperage setting will ever make MT work on aluminum.

sections.caption: Flux density is flux per unit area, and permeability is the slope B/H — steel develops thousands of times the flux density of air under the same magnetizing force.

sections.heading: The Law of Magnetism
sections.body:
The basic law of magnetism: **like poles repel, unlike poles attract**, with a force that is strongest close to the poles and falls off rapidly with distance.

Two corollaries matter:

- **Poles always come in pairs.** Cut a bar magnet in half and you do not get a loose north and a loose south — you get two complete smaller magnets, each with both poles. No matter how finely you divide it (down to a single domain), every piece has a north and a south. There is no magnetic monopole.
- **Attraction concentrates at poles.** Flux crosses the surface at poles, so that is where the pulling force on ferromagnetic material lives.

Now connect it to the job. A discontinuity in a magnetized part creates a tiny north–south pole pair at its edges. Those poles attract ferromagnetic particles exactly the way a magnet grabs iron filings — and because the force fades fast with distance, particles are held *tightly* at the flaw and only loosely elsewhere. That is why a real indication is sharp and crisp, sitting directly over the discontinuity, instead of smeared across the part.

sections.title: No domains, no MT
sections.body: MT works **only on ferromagnetic materials** — iron, nickel, cobalt, and their magnetic alloys. Before you test, confirm the material and grade (a 410 stainless valve body tests fine; a 304 body never will), and confirm the part is below the Curie point. When the material is not ferromagnetic, the answer is a different method, never a higher amperage.

figures.title: Magnetic domains before and after magnetization

interactive.title: Sort the Materials
interactive.intro: Classify each material by its magnetic behavior. Remember: MT is only possible on the first group, no matter what amperage you have available.

items.label: Low-carbon structural steel
items.why: Iron-based with a strong domain structure and high permeability — the classic MT material.

items.label: Nickel
items.why: One of the three ferromagnetic elements: iron, nickel, cobalt.

items.label: Cobalt
items.why: A ferromagnetic element — its atoms lock into aligned domains.

items.label: Hardened 4340 alloy steel
items.why: Ferromagnetic low-alloy steel; its high retentivity also makes it a candidate for the residual method.

items.label: Martensitic 410 stainless steel
items.why: The 400-series martensitic stainless grades are ferromagnetic and routinely tested with MT.

items.label: Aluminum
items.why: Weakly attracted with relative permeability barely above 1 — it cannot carry the flux MT needs.

items.label: Austenitic 304 stainless steel
items.why: The austenitic structure is essentially nonmagnetic (paramagnetic), so MT does not apply — check the grade, not the word stainless.

items.label: Titanium
items.why: Weakly paramagnetic — inspect it with PT or ET instead.

items.label: Copper
items.why: Slightly repelled by a field, relative permeability just under 1 — though it conducts current beautifully, which is why central conductors are often copper.

items.label: Bismuth
items.why: The classic strongly diamagnetic metal — visibly repelled by a powerful magnet.

checkQuestions.question: In an unmagnetized piece of steel, the magnetic domains are:
options: Absent until a magnetizing current is applied
Present and individually magnetized, but randomly oriented so their fields cancel
All aligned with the earth's magnetic field
Concentrated only at the surface of the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Domains exist in ferromagnetic material at all times; each is fully magnetized. In the unmagnetized state they point randomly and cancel — magnetizing the part aligns them rather than creating them.

checkQuestions.question: A valve body stamped "304 stainless" arrives with a work order calling for MT. The correct assessment is:
options: Ferromagnetic — proceed with MT as usual
Diamagnetic — it will repel the particles
Paramagnetic — MT is not applicable; the job needs another method such as PT
Ferromagnetic only if heated above the Curie point first
checkQuestions.explanation: Austenitic 300-series stainless is paramagnetic and cannot be tested with MT, while martensitic 400-series grades are ferromagnetic and can. Always verify the grade — and heating above the Curie point removes ferromagnetism, never creates it.

checkQuestions.question: A material develops a flux density of 12,000 gauss under a magnetizing force of 60 oersteds. Its permeability (µ = B/H) is about:
checkQuestions.explanation: Permeability is flux density divided by magnetizing force: 12,000 ÷ 60 = 200. Air under the same 60 oersteds would carry about 60 gauss (µ ≈ 1) — that ratio is why flux concentrates in steel.

checkQuestions.question: A carbon-steel forging is sitting at about 800 °C after heat treatment. Its magnetic condition is:
options: Above the Curie point (≈770 °C) — paramagnetic, and it cannot be magnetized until it cools
Ferromagnetic, exactly as at room temperature
checkQuestions.explanation: Above the Curie point (about 770 °C for iron), thermal agitation destroys domain alignment and the steel behaves paramagnetically. Ferromagnetism returns when the part cools below the Curie temperature.

checkQuestions.question: A bar magnet is sawed in half across its middle. The result is:
options: An isolated north pole and an isolated south pole
One piece with both poles and one piece with none
Two complete magnets, each with its own north and south pole
checkQuestions.explanation: Poles always exist in pairs — every division of a magnet, down to a single domain, yields a complete magnet. The same pairing is why a crack creates a north–south pole pair whose local field traps particles.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §2.2 (theory of magnetism)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §2.2.1–2.2.4 (domain theory, materials, flux density, law of magnetism)
Practices such as ASTM E709 and E1444 limit MT to ferromagnetic materials (generic reference)
Reference values: 10,000 gauss = 1 tesla; iron Curie point ≈ 770 °C
